title: Sjögren Syndrome
oneLiner: Autoimmune exocrinopathy causing sicca (dry eyes/mouth) ± systemic involvement; increased lymphoma risk; often secondary to RA/SLE.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Lymphoma transformation** – rapidly enlarging unilateral parotid/submandibular gland + B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) + rising LDH. *Single finding: firm, fixed, painless gland > 2 weeks, not responding to steroids.*
- **Cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis** – palpable purpura, mononeuritis multiplex, acute kidney injury, low C4. *Single finding: mononeuritis (foot drop, wrist drop) + purpura.*
- **Severe hypokalaemia from distal renal tubular acidosis (RTA)** – generalised weakness, ECG changes (U waves, T inversion), paralysis. *Single finding: non-anion gap metabolic acidosis with urine pH >5.5 and K+ <2.5.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Xerophthalmia (gritty, sandy feeling; photosensitivity; difficulty reading)
  - Xerostomia (difficulty swallowing dry food, need to sip water constantly, increased dental caries)
  - Recurrent bilateral parotid swelling (often non-tender, elastic)
  - Fatigue and arthralgias (common but non-specific)
  - Vaginal dryness, dry skin, dry cough (sicca symptoms)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced tear meniscus, corneal staining (fluorescein)
  - Dry, cracked tongue, loss of papillae, multiple dental caries
  - Bilateral parotid enlargement often smooth and non-tender
  - Palpable purpura (vasculitis), splinter haemorrhages
  - Joint swelling or tenderness (if secondary to RA/SLE)
  - Assess for peripheral neuropathy (vibration, pinprick, reflexes)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely → less likely:
  - **Drug-induced sicca** – anticholinergics, antidepressants, antihistamines, diuretics → resolves on stopping.
  - **IgG4-related disease** – bilateral parotid/lacrimal swelling, elevated IgG4, negative Ro/La, often with other organ involvement (pancreatitis, retroperitoneal fibrosis).
  - **Sarcoidosis** – bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, elevated ACE, non-caseating granulomas on biopsy.
  - **Viral sialadenitis (mumps, HIV, hepatitis C)** – acute unilateral swelling, fever, serology positive.
  - **Amyloidosis** – macroglossia, periorbital purpura, organomegaly, biopsy with Congo red.
  - **Primary Sjögren** – positive anti-Ro/SSA or anti-La/SSB, positive Schirmer test, salivary gland biopsy with focal lymphocytic sialadenitis.
- **Splitter:** *Bilateral parotid swelling + positive anti-Ro/La → Sjögren; bilateral parotid swelling + elevated IgG4 + negative Ro/La → IgG4-related disease (check IgG4 levels).*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Schirmer test (<5 mm wetting after 5 minutes suggests dry eye)
  - Salivary flow measurement (unstimulated whole saliva <1.5 mL in 15 minutes)
  - Urine dipstick (for pH, protein, blood – RTA or glomerulonephritis)
  - ECG if K+ low
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP/ESR
  - ANA (usually positive, speckled pattern), anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB
  - Rheumatoid factor (often positive)
  - Complement C3, C4 (low C4 suggests cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis)
  - Cryoglobulins (if purpura, neuropathy, renal involvement)
  - Immunoglobulins (IgG4 if suspected)
  - LDH (lymphoma screen)
  - Viral serology: HIV, HCV, EBV (if risk)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Ultrasound of salivary glands (heterogeneous parenchyma, cysts, calcifications; also excludes abscess/tumour)
  - **Second-line:** MRI sialography (if ultrasound inconclusive or suspicion of lymphoma); Chest HRCT (if pulmonary symptoms: interstitial lung disease)
- **Special tests:**
  - Minor salivary gland biopsy (from lower lip) – gold standard; focus score ≥1 (≥50 lymphocytes per 4 mm²) confirms diagnosis
  - Lip biopsy or labial gland biopsy (if anti-Ro/La negative and high suspicion)
- **Diagnostic criteria (2016 ACR-EULAR):**
  - Entry: subjective dry eyes/mouth + objective evidence (Schirmer/flow/biopsy autoantibodies)
  - Weighted score: anti-Ro/La (3), biopsy (3), Schirmer (1), abnormal sialometry (1)
  - If total ≥4 → classifiable as primary Sjögren
- **Never-miss test:** *Minor salivary gland biopsy* – do not start immunosuppression without histology if diagnosis uncertain, because gland swelling could be lymphoma or IgG4.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected lymphoma: urgent non-contrast CT of neck/chest/abdomen, arrange biopsy (core needle or excisional)
  - If hypokalaemia from RTA: K+ replacement (IV/PO) ± oral bicarbonate (NaHCO3) – [check local policy for dose]
  - If vasculitic crisis: check cryoglobulins, start high-dose prednisolone (1 mg/kg) after appropriate cultures; involve rheumatology immediately
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Symptomatic sicca: artificial tears (e.g., hypromellose 0.3% QDS), ocular lubricant gel at night; saliva substitutes (e.g., Saliva Orthana spray); consider pilocarpine (5 mg TDS/QDS) after checking asthma/glaucoma status – *do not exceed 10 mg QDS*
  - For arthralgia/myalgia: hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg daily, max 6.5 mg/kg lean body weight) – requires baseline eye screening
  - If organ-threatening disease (vasculitis, CNS, pulmonary, nephritis): methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg tapering; add rituximab (1 g IV on days 1 and 15) [check local protocol]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Hydroxychloroquine maintenance for systemic symptoms
  - Annual screening: lymphoma (examine gland size, LDH, U/S), ocular (corneal staining, visual fields if on HCQ), renal (U&E, urine pH, bicarbonate), dental
  - Vaccinations: influenza, pneumococcal, shingles, COVID-19 (as immune suppression may be used)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann's at maintenance rate if NBM; adjust if RTA (consider bicarbonate)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (or 20 mg if weight <50 kg) – if no bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal with frequent sips of water; consider soft diet if xerostomia severe
  - Observations: NEWS2 2-hourly initially; 4-hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Pilocarpine** 5 mg TDS with meals, increase to 5 mg QDS if needed [BNF licensed] – contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma, narrow-angle glaucoma, pregnancy.
- **Hydroxychloroquine** 200-400 mg daily (max 6.5 mg/kg lean body weight) – *screen for retinopathy at baseline and annually after 5 years.*
- **Prednisolone** 0.5-1 mg/kg daily for flares, taper over weeks – *check bone protection (alendronic acid, calcium/Vit D).*
- **Rituximab** 1 g IV days 1 and 15, then every 6 months [check local protocol] – *premedicate with paracetamol, chlorphenamine, methylprednisolone.*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cevimeline 30 mg TDS (if pilocarpine not tolerated, but limited UK availability)
  - Methotrexate (10-15 mg once weekly) for refractory synovitis
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Pilocarpine + beta-blockers: risk of bradycardia.
  - HCQ + QT-prolonging drugs: risk of torsades.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Pilocarpine: caution in hepatic impairment – use lower dose.
  - HCQ: adjust if eGFR <30 (reduce to 200 mg daily) [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Pilocarpine – start too high (10 mg) → bradycardia, hypotension, bronchospasm; always start 5 mg and titrate.*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Lymphoma (non-Hodgkin, usually MALT) – incidence 5-10%
  - Cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis – purpura, neuropathy, glomerulonephritis
  - Severe hypokalaemia from RTA – cardiac arrhythmia, paralysis
  - Interstitial lung disease (acute exacerbation)
  - Acute sialadenitis (infection of obstructed gland)
- **Chronic:**
  - Dental caries and tooth loss
  - Corneal ulceration, perforation
  - Pulmonary fibrosis
  - Peripheral neuropathy (sensory or mononeuritis multiplex)
  - Renal tubular acidosis (chronic)
  - Secondary Sjögren associated with RA, SLE, systemic sclerosis
sections.Prognosis: - Good for quality of life if sicca managed symptomatically; but 5-10% develop lymphoma – leading cause of death. Overall mortality not significantly increased without lymphoma or severe systemic disease. Chronic but stable with treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check for cryoglobulins** in any Sjögren patient with palpable purpura, neuropathy, or renal impairment – early detection prevents irreversible damage.
- **Perform annual lymphoma screening:** palpate glands, check LDH, and do neck U/S if any change.
- **Refer all newly diagnosed patients to ophthalmology for baseline Schirmer and corneal assessment –** prevents sight-threatening keratopathy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe hydroxychloroquine without baseline retinal screening** – toxicity is irreversible and cumulative dose-dependent.
- **Do not ignore unilateral parotid swelling** – it is a red flag for lymphoma or infection; never assume it’s just a flare.
- **Do not use systemic corticosteroids for sicca symptoms alone** – reserved for organ-threatening disease; use local measures first.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Rapid swelling of one gland (possible lymphoma)
  - New purpura, numbness, or weakness (vasculitis)
  - Fever, night sweats, weight loss (B symptoms)
  - Palpitations, muscle cramps (hypokalaemia)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Rheumatology routine clinic in 4-6 weeks
  - Ophthalmology within 1 month
  - Primary care: annual bloods, dental check
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurology
  - Acute kidney injury (creatinine rise >50%)
  - New purpura + renal impairment (suspect vasculitis)
  - Serum K+ <2.5 mmol/L
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Respiratory failure (SpO2 <90% on O2)
  - Seizure or cardiac arrest
  - Rapidly progressive renal failure with oliguria

rawMarkdown:
## Sjögren Syndrome

**One-liner:** Autoimmune exocrinopathy causing sicca (dry eyes/mouth) ± systemic involvement; increased lymphoma risk; often secondary to RA/SLE.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Lymphoma transformation** – rapidly enlarging unilateral parotid/submandibular gland + B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) + rising LDH. *Single finding: firm, fixed, painless gland > 2 weeks, not responding to steroids.*
- **Cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis** – palpable purpura, mononeuritis multiplex, acute kidney injury, low C4. *Single finding: mononeuritis (foot drop, wrist drop) + purpura.*
- **Severe hypokalaemia from distal renal tubular acidosis (RTA)** – generalised weakness, ECG changes (U waves, T inversion), paralysis. *Single finding: non-anion gap metabolic acidosis with urine pH >5.5 and K+ <2.5.*

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Xerophthalmia (gritty, sandy feeling; photosensitivity; difficulty reading)
  - Xerostomia (difficulty swallowing dry food, need to sip water constantly, increased dental caries)
  - Recurrent bilateral parotid swelling (often non-tender, elastic)
  - Fatigue and arthralgias (common but non-specific)
  - Vaginal dryness, dry skin, dry cough (sicca symptoms)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced tear meniscus, corneal staining (fluorescein)
  - Dry, cracked tongue, loss of papillae, multiple dental caries
  - Bilateral parotid enlargement often smooth and non-tender
  - Palpable purpura (vasculitis), splinter haemorrhages
  - Joint swelling or tenderness (if secondary to RA/SLE)
  - Assess for peripheral neuropathy (vibration, pinprick, reflexes)

### Differential Diagnosis

- Most likely → less likely:
  - **Drug-induced sicca** – anticholinergics, antidepressants, antihistamines, diuretics → resolves on stopping.
  - **IgG4-related disease** – bilateral parotid/lacrimal swelling, elevated IgG4, negative Ro/La, often with other organ involvement (pancreatitis, retroperitoneal fibrosis).
  - **Sarcoidosis** – bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, elevated ACE, non-caseating granulomas on biopsy.
  - **Viral sialadenitis (mumps, HIV, hepatitis C)** – acute unilateral swelling, fever, serology positive.
  - **Amyloidosis** – macroglossia, periorbital purpura, organomegaly, biopsy with Congo red.
  - **Primary Sjögren** – positive anti-Ro/SSA or anti-La/SSB, positive Schirmer test, salivary gland biopsy with focal lymphocytic sialadenitis.
- **Splitter:** *Bilateral parotid swelling + positive anti-Ro/La → Sjögren; bilateral parotid swelling + elevated IgG4 + negative Ro/La → IgG4-related disease (check IgG4 levels).*

### Investigations

- **Bedside:**
  - Schirmer test (<5 mm wetting after 5 minutes suggests dry eye)
  - Salivary flow measurement (unstimulated whole saliva <1.5 mL in 15 minutes)
  - Urine dipstick (for pH, protein, blood – RTA or glomerulonephritis)
  - ECG if K+ low
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP/ESR
  - ANA (usually positive, speckled pattern), anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB
  - Rheumatoid factor (often positive)
  - Complement C3, C4 (low C4 suggests cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis)
  - Cryoglobulins (if purpura, neuropathy, renal involvement)
  - Immunoglobulins (IgG4 if suspected)
  - LDH (lymphoma screen)
  - Viral serology: HIV, HCV, EBV (if risk)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Ultrasound of salivary glands (heterogeneous parenchyma, cysts, calcifications; also excludes abscess/tumour)
  - **Second-line:** MRI sialography (if ultrasound inconclusive or suspicion of lymphoma); Chest HRCT (if pulmonary symptoms: interstitial lung disease)
- **Special tests:**
  - Minor salivary gland biopsy (from lower lip) – gold standard; focus score ≥1 (≥50 lymphocytes per 4 mm²) confirms diagnosis
  - Lip biopsy or labial gland biopsy (if anti-Ro/La negative and high suspicion)
- **Diagnostic criteria (2016 ACR-EULAR):**
  - Entry: subjective dry eyes/mouth + objective evidence (Schirmer/flow/biopsy autoantibodies)
  - Weighted score: anti-Ro/La (3), biopsy (3), Schirmer (1), abnormal sialometry (1)
  - If total ≥4 → classifiable as primary Sjögren
- **Never-miss test:** *Minor salivary gland biopsy* – do not start immunosuppression without histology if diagnosis uncertain, because gland swelling could be lymphoma or IgG4.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected lymphoma: urgent non-contrast CT of neck/chest/abdomen, arrange biopsy (core needle or excisional)
  - If hypokalaemia from RTA: K+ replacement (IV/PO) ± oral bicarbonate (NaHCO3) – [check local policy for dose]
  - If vasculitic crisis: check cryoglobulins, start high-dose prednisolone (1 mg/kg) after appropriate cultures; involve rheumatology immediately
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Symptomatic sicca: artificial tears (e.g., hypromellose 0.3% QDS), ocular lubricant gel at night; saliva substitutes (e.g., Saliva Orthana spray); consider pilocarpine (5 mg TDS/QDS) after checking asthma/glaucoma status – *do not exceed 10 mg QDS*
  - For arthralgia/myalgia: hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg daily, max 6.5 mg/kg lean body weight) – requires baseline eye screening
  - If organ-threatening disease (vasculitis, CNS, pulmonary, nephritis): methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg tapering; add rituximab (1 g IV on days 1 and 15) [check local protocol]
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Hydroxychloroquine maintenance for systemic symptoms
  - Annual screening: lymphoma (examine gland size, LDH, U/S), ocular (corneal staining, visual fields if on HCQ), renal (U&E, urine pH, bicarbonate), dental
  - Vaccinations: influenza, pneumococcal, shingles, COVID-19 (as immune suppression may be used)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline or Hartmann's at maintenance rate if NBM; adjust if RTA (consider bicarbonate)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (or 20 mg if weight <50 kg) – if no bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal with frequent sips of water; consider soft diet if xerostomia severe
  - Observations: NEWS2 2-hourly initially; 4-hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses

- **Pilocarpine** 5 mg TDS with meals, increase to 5 mg QDS if needed [BNF licensed] – contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma, narrow-angle glaucoma, pregnancy.
- **Hydroxychloroquine** 200-400 mg daily (max 6.5 mg/kg lean body weight) – *screen for retinopathy at baseline and annually after 5 years.*
- **Prednisolone** 0.5-1 mg/kg daily for flares, taper over weeks – *check bone protection (alendronic acid, calcium/Vit D).*
- **Rituximab** 1 g IV days 1 and 15, then every 6 months [check local protocol] – *premedicate with paracetamol, chlorphenamine, methylprednisolone.*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cevimeline 30 mg TDS (if pilocarpine not tolerated, but limited UK availability)
  - Methotrexate (10-15 mg once weekly) for refractory synovitis
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Pilocarpine + beta-blockers: risk of bradycardia.
  - HCQ + QT-prolonging drugs: risk of torsades.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Pilocarpine: caution in hepatic impairment – use lower dose.
  - HCQ: adjust if eGFR <30 (reduce to 200 mg daily) [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Pilocarpine – start too high (10 mg) → bradycardia, hypotension, bronchospasm; always start 5 mg and titrate.*

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Lymphoma (non-Hodgkin, usually MALT) – incidence 5-10%
  - Cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis – purpura, neuropathy, glomerulonephritis
  - Severe hypokalaemia from RTA – cardiac arrhythmia, paralysis
  - Interstitial lung disease (acute exacerbation)
  - Acute sialadenitis (infection of obstructed gland)
- **Chronic:**
  - Dental caries and tooth loss
  - Corneal ulceration, perforation
  - Pulmonary fibrosis
  - Peripheral neuropathy (sensory or mononeuritis multiplex)
  - Renal tubular acidosis (chronic)
  - Secondary Sjögren associated with RA, SLE, systemic sclerosis

### Prognosis

- Good for quality of life if sicca managed symptomatically; but 5-10% develop lymphoma – leading cause of death. Overall mortality not significantly increased without lymphoma or severe systemic disease. Chronic but stable with treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always check for cryoglobulins** in any Sjögren patient with palpable purpura, neuropathy, or renal impairment – early detection prevents irreversible damage.
- **Perform annual lymphoma screening:** palpate glands, check LDH, and do neck U/S if any change.
- **Refer all newly diagnosed patients to ophthalmology for baseline Schirmer and corneal assessment –** prevents sight-threatening keratopathy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do not prescribe hydroxychloroquine without baseline retinal screening** – toxicity is irreversible and cumulative dose-dependent.
- **Do not ignore unilateral parotid swelling** – it is a red flag for lymphoma or infection; never assume it’s just a flare.
- **Do not use systemic corticosteroids for sicca symptoms alone** – reserved for organ-threatening disease; use local measures first.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**
  - Rapid swelling of one gland (possible lymphoma)
  - New purpura, numbness, or weakness (vasculitis)
  - Fever, night sweats, weight loss (B symptoms)
  - Palpitations, muscle cramps (hypokalaemia)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Rheumatology routine clinic in 4-6 weeks
  - Ophthalmology within 1 month
  - Primary care: annual bloods, dental check

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurology
  - Acute kidney injury (creatinine rise >50%)
  - New purpura + renal impairment (suspect vasculitis)
  - Serum K+ <2.5 mmol/L
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Respiratory failure (SpO2 <90% on O2)
  - Seizure or cardiac arrest
  - Rapidly progressive renal failure with oliguria
